summary:
Evidence from the PPNA site of [[event:1311|Gilgal]] in the lower Jordan Valley, dated to approximately 9400 BCE, suggests that cultivated parthenocarpic (seedless) figs were being grown there — making this possibly the world's earliest documented example of deliberate fruit tree cultivation. Parthenocarpic figs, unlike wild figs, cannot reproduce without human intervention since they require transplanting of cuttings or suckers, meaning their presence in the archaeological record necessarily implies human cultivation.

The cultivation of figs in the Jordan Valley apparently slightly predated the full domestication of the cereal crops that are usually considered the centerpieces of early Neolithic agriculture. This finding broadens the picture of early agricultural activity, suggesting that early Neolithic communities were simultaneously experimenting with multiple plant species — including tree crops requiring years to bear fruit — not just quickly-maturing annual grain crops. Figs provided an important source of sugar, dried fruit for storage, and dietary calories in the early Neolithic diet. They remained an important crop in the ancient Near East and Mediterranean throughout antiquity, featured prominently in Egyptian, Mesopotamian, Greek, and Roman foodways.
